# Phase 2 - The Geometric Structure: Geometric Deep Learning

## Introduction

Phase 1 gave you the engine. You can now measure the distance between probability distributions with geometric precision, compute that distance efficiently through the Sinkhorn algorithm, interpolate between distributions along geodesics, and average collections of distributions through Wasserstein barycenters. The mathematical machinery of Optimal Transport is complete, and you have seen it deployed across the full range from pure theory to numerical implementation.

Phase 2 builds the chassis — the neural network architectures that can operate on the kinds of data that the real world actually produces.

Here is the problem that Phase 2 addresses. Every neural network architecture you have encountered in standard machine learning courses — the multilayer perceptron, the convolutional network, the transformer — is designed for data that lives on a grid. Images are grids of pixels. Sequences are grids of tokens. Tabular data is a grid of features. The mathematical operations that these architectures perform — matrix multiplications, convolutions, attention mechanisms — are operations on vectors and matrices, objects that live in flat Euclidean space. They assume that the input data is a fixed-length vector, that the coordinates of that vector have a fixed meaning, and that the space in which the data lives is flat and homogeneous.

The real world does not produce grid data. A molecule is a set of atoms with three-dimensional coordinates and chemical bonds — a graph embedded in three-dimensional space. A protein backbone is a sequence of amino acids whose spatial configuration is a curve on a Riemannian manifold of dihedral angles. A social network is a graph whose structure encodes relationships, not positions. A 3D point cloud from a LiDAR sensor is an unordered set of points in $\mathbb{R}^3$ with no canonical ordering and no grid structure. A weather system is a distribution of physical quantities on the surface of a sphere — a non-flat manifold. None of these objects can be honestly represented as a fixed-length Euclidean vector without destroying the structure that makes them meaningful.

The standard response to this problem — flatten everything into a vector and feed it to a generic neural network — is computationally wasteful and statistically dishonest. It is wasteful because it ignores the geometric structure of the data, forcing the network to rediscover from scratch the invariances and symmetries that are built into the problem. A molecule's energy does not change when you rotate the molecule — the network should know this without being trained to learn it from examples. It is dishonest because the flattened representation conflates things that are geometrically identical. Two representations of the same molecule, one rotated by ninety degrees, look completely different as vectors, even though they represent the same physical object with the same properties.

Geometric Deep Learning is the systematic answer to this problem. Its central insight — articulated by Bronstein, Bruna, LeCun, Szlam, and Vandergheynst in the Geometric Deep Learning blueprint, and formalized as the Erlangen Program of deep learning — is that neural network architecture should be determined by the symmetry group of the data. Every dataset has a group of transformations that leave the relevant properties unchanged. For images, the relevant symmetry is translation — shifting an image does not change what it depicts. For molecules, the relevant symmetry is the Euclidean group $E(3)$ — rotating and translating a molecule does not change its energy or its chemical properties. For graphs representing social networks, the relevant symmetry is the permutation group $S_n$ — relabeling the nodes does not change the graph's structural properties. For data on the sphere, the relevant symmetry is the rotation group $SO(3)$.

The architectural principle of Geometric Deep Learning is: build neural networks whose computations respect these symmetries exactly — not approximately, not statistically, but exactly. A network that is equivariant to rotations produces outputs that rotate when the input is rotated. A network that is invariant to permutations produces the same output regardless of how the graph's nodes are labeled. Respecting symmetry exactly means the network never needs to learn the symmetry from data — it is built in at the architectural level, freeing all of the network's capacity for learning the actual task.

Phase 2 develops this program across three chapters, each one focused on a different geometric setting.

The first chapter is **The Erlangen Program of Deep Learning** — the theoretical foundation that connects group theory to neural network architecture. The chapter covers the precise definitions of invariance and equivariance, the mathematical relationship between groups and the constraints they impose on neural computations, and the two most important symmetry groups in practice: the permutation group $S_n$ for graph-structured data and the rotation group $SO(3)$ for three-dimensional geometric data. This chapter provides the conceptual language — invariance, equivariance, group action, representation — that makes the subsequent chapters precise rather than heuristic.

The second chapter is **Graph Neural Networks** — the family of neural network architectures designed for graph-structured data, respecting the permutation symmetry of graph nodes. The chapter covers the message passing framework — the spatial view of graph convolution as iterative aggregation of neighborhood information — the spectral view via the graph Laplacian and ChebNet — and the theoretical analysis of GNN expressive power through the Weisfeiler-Lehman isomorphism test, which characterizes exactly which graph structural properties a message-passing GNN can and cannot detect. Graph neural networks are the workhorse architecture for molecules, social networks, knowledge graphs, and any other data with relational structure.

The third chapter is **Manifold Learning and Curvature** — the generalization of graph convolution to data that lives on smooth curved surfaces rather than discrete graphs. The chapter covers geodesic convolution — the extension of the convolutional operation to curved surfaces using locally defined coordinate systems — and Laplacian eigenmaps — the embedding of high-dimensional manifold-valued data into low-dimensional Euclidean space using the eigenfunctions of the Laplace-Beltrami operator. These tools connect the differential geometry of Phase 0 to the neural network architectures of Phase 2, closing the loop from the abstract mathematics of manifolds and curvature to the concrete computations of deep learning.

By the end of Phase 2, you will be able to design neural network architectures from first principles for any structured data type — graphs, point clouds, manifold-valued data, three-dimensional geometric objects — by identifying the relevant symmetry group and constructing equivariant operations with respect to that group. The checkpoint project — building an E(3)-equivariant graph neural network from scratch and proving its equivariance by code — is the culmination of this program: a complete, working implementation of the most important symmetry-respecting architecture in computational chemistry and structural biology.

The trajectory of the full roadmap is now visible. Phase 0 gave you the mathematical language. Phase 1 gave you the geometric engine for distributions. Phase 2 gives you the architectural principles for geometric data. Phase 3 will synthesize all three — using the geometric architectures of Phase 2 to parameterize the generative flows of Phase 1, producing models that generate structured geometric data by learning to transport probability mass through the Wasserstein space of molecular structures, protein conformations, and three-dimensional shapes. The car is almost assembled. Phase 3 makes it drive.

---
---
